rationale: Authority data for W40K-0091..W40K-0100 — the 2017-2019 cluster bracketing the 8th-edition / Dark-Imperium pivot. Bookends: Andy Clark's *Shroud of Night* (2017, Alpha Legion infiltration on the oceanic hive world Tsadrekha during the Noctis Aeterna, Khârn the Betrayer-led rival assault, Unsung warband + Strike-Captain Kassar) and Andy Clark's *Celestine: The Living Saint* (2019, dual-strand novel about Saint Celestine in Imperium Nihilus + her allegorical torment-realm). Heart of the batch: Robbie MacNiven's *The Last Hunt* (2017, White-Scars 4th Company under Joghaten Khan on Honour-World Darkand vs. Tyranids + Genestealer Cults + uneasy Aeldari/Drukhari alliance), Rob Sanders' *Sons of the Hydra* (2017, Alpha-Legion warband 'the Redacted' under Occam the Untrue, twisty Maelstrom infiltration), Josh Reynolds' *Lukas the Trickster* (2018, Space-Wolves trickster-duel between Lukas the Strifeson and Drukhari Duke Sliscus on Fenris during Helwinter), Gav Thorpe's *Imperator: Wrath of the Omnissiah* (2018, Legio Metalica's Imperator-titan *Casus Belli* + Magos Exasus on Nicomedua — 'Hunt for Red October with an Imperator Titan'), Mike Lee's *Legacy of Dorn* (2018, Crimson-Fists Veteran-Sergeant Sandor Galleas guerrilla campaign during the ork invasion of Rynn's World), Robbie MacNiven's *Blood of Iax* (2018, Primaris-only Ultramarines Strike Force Fulminata — brothers Chaplain Kastor + Apothecary Polixis — vs Warlord Urgork on Ikara IX in the Dark-Imperium era), Chris Wraight's *The Lords of Silence* (2018, Death-Guard warband under First-Captain Vorx + Slert + Philemon, warp-stranded grand-cruiser *Solace*, 13th-Black-Crusade-aimed-at-Ultramar setup, whodunnit register), and Aaron Dembski-Bowden's *Spear of the Emperor* (2018, helot-narrator Anuradha relaying Mentor-Legion Lieutenant Amadeus Kaias Incarius's crossing of the Great Rift into the Elara's Veil nebula to assess the Emperor's Spears + Star Scorpions + Celestial Lions chapters). Structural notes: dense first-authority-layer surface-form additions (Alpha Legion as primary protagonist faction, Death Guard as primary protagonist faction, Crimson Fists chapter as primary protagonist faction, White Scars chapter as primary protagonist faction, Death-Guard-/Nurgle-/Word-Bearers Chaos surface forms, Primaris-Astartes register, Tsadrekha / Darkand / Nicomedua / Rynn's World / Ikara IX / Elara's Veil + Solace / Casus Belli named-vessel surface forms, Khârn the Betrayer + Duke Sliscus + Urgork + Vorx + Lukas the Strifeson + Galleas + Kastor + Polixis + Amadeus + Anuradha + Celestine + Magos Exasus + Joghaten Khan + Occam the Untrue + Kassar + Slert + Philemon character surface forms). One `data_conflict { field: format, suggestion: short_novel/novella }` flag on Celestine (Lexicanum + Track-of-Words consistently characterise it as 'short novel' / novella-length; the SSOT format=`novel` is plausibly correct but worth flagging). No `low_confidence` flags this iteration — all ten entries have strong post-2017 Black-Library coverage. **Resolver-Pre-Warning note:** cumulativeBefore=90 (Brief-061 algorithm's strict reading: 90 % 50 ≠ 0 → no pause); after this batch, cumulativeAfter=100, which DOES trigger the loud-stop in the NEXT iteration (`ssot-w40k-011`). User explicitly requested this batch, so produced normally; the resolver-pause-block will fire on the subsequent iteration.

overrides.synopsis: Standalone Chaos-anchored novel set during the Noctis Aeterna in Imperium Nihilus. On the oceanic hive world of Tsadrekha, the planetary Astropathic beacon — a unique psychic light that holds back the warp-storm darkness now blanketing the region — has become the prize every Heretic-Astartes warband in the Maelstrom is converging on. Sent by his patron warlord to seize and corrupt the beacon, Strike-Captain Kassar leads the Unsung — an elite Alpha-Legion infiltration warband stranded on a daemon world since the days of the Scouring — in a desperate bid for both the prize and their own freedom from exile. Their cover is wrecked when a brutal frontal assault led by Khârn the Betrayer and his World-Eaters Berserkers turns Tsadrekha into an open battlefield: Khornate, Slaaneshi, Tzeentchian and rival Alpha-Legion warbands all close in simultaneously. On the Imperial side, an Imperial Saint, Tsadrekhan Guardsmen, Adepta Sororitas detachments, and an Imperial-Fists strike force fight to hold the beacon while the Unsung run their layered infiltration plan. Characteristic twisty Alpha-Legion register: nobody on either side is what they claim to be, and Kassar's freedom hinges on whether the warband can play every Chaos faction off against the others before the Imperial defence finishes them. **First authority-layer entry where Alpha Legion is the primary protagonist faction.**

Standalone White-Scars chapter novel set on the steppe Honour-World of Darkand in the immediate aftermath of the Great Rift's opening — the early Gathering-Storm window in which Roboute Guilliman's return is still rumour on the Imperium's frontier and the Astronomican's signal is intermittent at best. A splinter Tyranid fleet from Hive Fleet Leviathan, vanguarded by Genestealer-Cult infiltrators that have already compromised significant fractions of Darkand's PDF and aristocracy, strikes the world the White Scars are oath-sworn to protect. Joghaten Khan leads the chapter's 4th Company in answering the call. The campaign turns from straightforward bug-stomp to triangulated war when an Aeldari Craftworld force and a Drukhari Kabal — pursuing their own targets on Darkand for reasons that have nothing to do with the Imperium — make contact and offer an uneasy alliance. The trust question becomes the structural spine of the novel: the Aeldari's offer is plausibly real, but the Khan has to weigh chapter-honour and the chapter's blood-debt to Darkand against the well-documented xenos history of using Imperial proxies to fight xenos wars. **First authority-layer entry where White Scars is the primary protagonist faction.**

Standalone Alpha-Legion warband novel and loose follow-up to *Shroud of Night*. Occam the Untrue — a Strike-Captain operating out of the Maelstrom — leads his small, doctrinally-mixed warband, the Redacted (Alpha-Legionnaires plus renegade-recruit Astartes from a half-dozen other Chapters and a Hydra cult of human auxiliaries), on a long-range operation that begins as an Alpha-Legion infiltration job and unfolds into something neither Occam nor his immediate masters anticipated. Inquisition strike-forces have been chasing the Redacted for months; the warband's cover is paper-thin, every member is potentially a plant from a rival legion or a double-agent of one of the Patriarchs Above, and Occam himself is no longer certain his orders are coming from the Legion proper. The plot runs the characteristic Alpha-Legion twisty-onion register — false faces and false objectives stacked on top of each other — and culminates in a confrontation at the cold heart of the galaxy that recontextualises the Redacted's mission and Occam's own loyalties. Plasma-heavy combat texture; deception-as-warfare register; a quiet Cabal-undercurrent suggests the warband may have been pieces on a galactic-scale Alpha-Legion board from the start. **First authority-layer entry where the Alpha Legion's twisty-infiltration register is the primary plot mode** (vs. *Shroud of Night*'s mostly-frontal Tsadrekha-siege register).

Standalone Space-Wolves novel built around Lukas the Strifeson — the chapter's iconic Jackalwolf, a Blood Claw whose pranks, insolence, and tactical genius have made him unwelcome in every Wolf Lord's Great Company in turn. The book opens during Helwinter on Fenris, when Wolf-Priest Galerunner reassigns Lukas for the umpteenth time, this time to Wolf-Lord Kjarl Grimblood's Great Company. The reassignment ritual is barely over when a Drukhari raider fleet under the corsair-archon Duke Traevelliath Sliscus, the Serpent of Commorragh, makes planetfall on Fenris in pursuit of trophy-flesh and the trophy of *killing* the legendary Wolves themselves. Lukas, demoted-because-promoted to leading Grimblood's Blood Claws, runs a campaign of escalating ambushes and counter-traps through the Fenrisian back-country, joined by the surviving native Fenrisian tribes; Sliscus, who senses a rival worth his time, escalates in kind. The novel braids two register-layers: the trickster-vs-trickster duel between Lukas and Sliscus as a kind of folk-tale-of-the-Wolf, and the chapter-internal politics of what to do with a battle-brother whose value is genius and whose cost is morale. **First authority-layer entry where the Drukhari (post-rename Dark Eldar) surface form appears as a primary antagonist faction** (prior batches used 'Dark Eldar' for pre-rename publications); **first explicit Duke Sliscus surface form.**

Standalone Adeptus-Titanicus novel built around an Imperator-class Titan. The *Casus Belli*, an Imperator-pattern god-machine of Legio Metalica that has served the Omnissiah for over ten thousand years, leads a Mechanicus battlegroup of Warlord and Reaver Titans, Imperial-Knight households, and skitarii cohorts onto the forge-world Nicomedua, where a tech-priest-led secession has broken out under the cover of the Cicatrix Maledictum. While the *Casus Belli* and its escort engage corrupted Knights, traitor Titans, traitor-Astartes detachments and cultist hordes in open city-after-city war, Magos Exasus — commander of the *Casus Belli*'s Tech-guard contingent — must hunt the saboteur(s) embedded inside the Imperator's own crew-hierarchy before the war on the ground is undone by the war on the inside. Pitched by Gav Thorpe as 'Hunt for Red October with an Imperator Titan' — naval-mystery procedural housed inside a city-scale Titan war. **Third authority-layer Adeptus-Titanicus entry** (after W40K-0077 *Titanicus* in ssot-w40k-008 and W40K-0088 *Warlord* in ssot-w40k-009); **first explicit Imperator-class Titan surface form** and **first Legio Metalica surface form**.

Standalone Crimson-Fists chapter novel set during the ork invasion of Rynn's World — the same WAAAGH! that drives Steve Parker's *Rynn's World* and that effectively breaks the Crimson Fists as a full chapter. Veteran-Sergeant Sandor Galleas — Sternguard, chapter-pure in his conviction that Crimson Fists doctrine is just Imperial-Fists doctrine refined for a successor-chapter generation — leads his squad into orbital deployment over the planet at the precise moment the chapter's fortress-monastery Arx Tyrannus is destroyed and the chapter cut down to a remnant. Cut off behind ork lines with a battered band of human survivors from the planet's hive-cities, Galleas waives the chapter's siege-fortification doctrine and runs a hit-and-run guerrilla campaign through the wasteland and ruined-city terrain instead. Slow-burn attrition register: Galleas's Sternguard squad loses men week by week, the human auxiliaries are pushed past their endurance, and the chapter's survival hangs on whether Galleas can carry a guerrilla's resolve in a Crimson Fists uniform. **First authority-layer entry where the Crimson Fists is the primary protagonist faction** (Imperial-Fists-successor register distinct from W40K-0078 *Sons of Dorn*'s Imperial-Fists-primary register in ssot-w40k-008).

Standalone Ultramarines novel set in the Dark-Imperium era after the Great Rift, built around Strike Force Fulminata — a Primaris-only Ultramarines force operating on the ork-held mining world Ikara IX against a WAAAGH! led by Warlord Urgork. Dual-protagonist register through the blood-brothers Chaplain Kastor (fire-and-fury Astartes-priest, charges first and reasons after) and Apothecary Polixis (calm, pragmatic, gene-seed-disciplined). When the campaign turns against the Imperium and Urgork — cannier than orks are supposed to be — reveals a plan that hinges specifically on the Apothecary rather than on chapter-master-tier targets, the brotherhood between Kastor and Polixis becomes the load-bearing thread of the novel. Multi-POV chapters rotate through other Fulminata Astartes (notably including the Reivers, the Primaris-only terror-spec squad, with a structural look at how the wider Ultramarines view their tactics), an Astra-Militarum Lieutenant on Ikara's defence line, and a scattered handful of ork POVs. **First authority-layer Primaris-Astartes-anchored novel** and **first authority-layer Strike-Force Fulminata surface form**. The title's *Iax* refers metaphorically to the Ultramarines gene-line (Iax = Ultramar agri-world, ancestral chapter framing), not to the campaign's actual setting (Ikara IX).

Standalone Death-Guard novel built around the warband-of-the-same-name, commanded by First-Captain Vorx — a Heresy-era Death-Guard legionnaire whose tenure stretches back to Mortarion's pre-fall Barbarus and whose tactical register is methodical, numerologically-obsessed, quietly bitter. The Lords of Silence's grand cruiser, the *Solace*, is part of Abaddon the Despoiler's Thirteenth Black Crusade fleet aimed at Ultramar when a shipboard incident throws the *Solace* off-course into the deep warp; the book opens with the warband disoriented in a warp-system they cannot identify. Vorx, his Biologus Putrifier Slert, and his Tallyman Philemon must work out (a) where they are, (b) how to rejoin the Crusade, and (c) which of the warband's own number caused the *Solace* to jump — a Death-Guard whodunnit running in parallel to the strategic-rejoin question. En route they encounter a Word-Bearers vessel and broker an uneasy Chaos-Astartes alliance for the rejoin. The register is plague-monks-meets-Hercule-Poirot inside Astartes ranks; the structural payoff is that Death Guard, as a legion, are *patient* and *boring* in a way the other Chaos legions are not, and that patience is the thing that makes them implacable. **First authority-layer entry where Death Guard is the primary protagonist faction**, **first Nurgle Chaos-God surface form**, **first explicit Word-Bearers surface form**, and **first Abaddon the Despoiler / Thirteenth Black Crusade mentioned-frame** in the authority layer.

Standalone first-person memoir-framed Astartes novel narrated from decades after the events by Anuradha — helot (armourer / squire / valet hybrid) to Lieutenant Amadeus Kaias Incarius of the Mentors chapter. Amadeus is sent across the newly-opened Great Rift into the Elara's Veil nebula in Imperium Nihilus to assess the survival of three frontier-protector chapters: the Star Scorpions (genetically failing), the Celestial Lions (devastated by an Inquisition purge for sins they did not commit), and the Emperor's Spears (the only chapter still maintaining their vigil and now stretched far beyond what doctrine permits). Amadeus arrives as a Primaris emissary from the loyal-Imperium-proper into a frontier where the Imperium has effectively ceased to exist, finds his Mentors-chapter pedigree gets him a cold welcome from the Spears, and learns over the course of his crossing that there is no way back through the Great Rift. The novel's spine is Amadeus's gradual transition from observer-diplomat to fighting-brother of the Spears; Anuradha's helot-perspective lets the reader see the Astartes from outside their own cultural register — admiring, exasperated, occasionally horrified, decades-of-hindsight wise. **First authority-layer entry where the Emperor's Spears chapter is the primary protagonist faction**, **first explicit Mentors / Mentor Legion surface form**, **first Star Scorpions + Celestial Lions surface forms**, and **first long-form helot-POV register** (closest prior parallel is the servitor-POV intervals in W40K-0090 *Lucius* in ssot-w40k-009).

Standalone short novel built around Saint Celestine — the Adepta-Sororitas Living Saint, whose every battlefield-death drops her into a torment-realm in which her faith must be tested anew before she may return to fight again. Two parallel strands braid the novel: the outer strand follows Celestine and a Sororitas force in a desperate ground battle in Imperium Nihilus on the world where the saint manifests once more, fighting alongside Astra-Militarum line-troops and Imperial-fanatic civilians under the Noctis-Aeterna sky; the inner strand is the allegorical journey — Celestine's traversal of the torment-realm in which the cost of her gift is reckoned, and in which the question of whether faith *itself* is a corrupted thing surfaces as a slow philosophical horror. Set in the post-Cadia / Gathering-Storm window; standalone (does not directly continue *Shroud of Night*, though the two share thematic furniture). Cost-of-faith register; the saint's reward for centuries of dying-for-the-Emperor is more dying-for-the-Emperor. **First authority-layer entry where Adepta Sororitas is the primary protagonist faction** and **first explicit Saint Celestine surface form** (the unnamed Imperial Saint in W40K-0091 *Shroud of Night* may be Celestine in the same continuity but is not confirmed in summary sources).

flags.reason: Lexicanum + Track-of-Words + Black Library product page consistently characterise *Celestine* as a 'short novel' / novella-length release (closer to the *Assassinorum: Execution Force* register from ssot-w40k-009 than a standard novel).
